item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not bad, but it's really bland and lacking in anything resembling a personality. That is bad, so I'm downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An estimated 1,200,000kg of left ears are present, representing approximately 60 billion individuals

That makes it 50 000 ears per kilogram or 0,02 grams per ear. Are you sure these weren't ant antennae?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just hope that if it ever manifests a pile of keter cakes, it will vanish the additional duplicates when it moves on to the next think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My take on it was that the death was caused due to the loss of water, not things derived from the sugars or other constituents which in actuality comprise very little of the total content of soda. I would expect the loss of just the sugars to result in mutations possible resulting in tumors or autoimmune diseases, but those aren't instantly fatal. However, after 5 days a large portion of the body's water content would have been replaced with water from the sodas, and its sudden loss would definitely be immediately fatal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Laboratory mice are routinely given materials (especially sugar) dissolved in their water supply as part of experiments, at concentrations far greater than typically found in soda. In other parts of the world, people routinely drink water with high carbonic acid content. As long as food was also provided normally, a mouse would suffer no significant adverse affect from having its water supply replaced with coke over a mere 5 days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who thinks "ALL HAIL THE GLOW CLOUD" when reading this one?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty sure you've been told this before, Agent MacLeod, but there is nothing wrong with necroposting if the comment adds to the discussion. In this case, the comment is fine.

Please leave the calling out of users on their behavior to staff. You are coming off way too authoritative here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You do realize that you just responded to a post from a year and eleven months ago, right? We here at the SCP Foundation wiki call that "necroposting," and it's a big no-no. Look at the date of posts before responding to them, so you don't accidentally bring threads back from the dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I reckon it should be Keter, as there are many things which could appear which would constitute an XK scenario, and even more things which would do substantial harm. In view of this the site isn't really properly contained at the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The issue is that a lot of people think that the concept of race doesn't actually refer to something- e.g. that there is no such thing as biological race.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, here's my take on it.

+ Warning: Huge wall of text
– hide block

Of the ████ manifestations recorded by the Foundation to date, 76% have been man-made objects, and 16% have consisted of human or animal body parts.

Hmm… 76 and 16 still leaves out 8 % unaccounted for, and unless I overlooked a [DATA EXPUNGED] or other set of black boxes (which I have done a lot before); or I could just be overlooking something glaringly obvious, like 8 % unknown, but for now I'm assuming it was intentionally left out.

Counting four digits blacked out means that there have been somewhere between 80 and 800 things showing up that aren't man made or were parts of animals. And judging from the picture at the top, I'm assuming skulls and such fall under the category of human or animal body parts

No living organisms have been observed to manifest.

From here I'm assuming that either no cadavers have shown up untouched, or that they fall under the category of human or animal body parts again.

Objects Observed: Unknown
Notes: The previous manifestation disappeared as expected, but no new manifestation was discovered. It has been suggested that a manifestation may have in fact occurred, but the object or objects were small enough or inconspicuous enough to have escaped notice.

Interesting, I might be on to something here…

Objects Observed: SCP objects possessing or relating to [REDACTED] abilities

Bam. Wow. This isn't just an isolated case, it's happened before, and a lot. And to top it off:

The remaining eleven objects may represent SCP objects to which SCP-1183's researchers do not have clearance to access, or they may represent objects which are not currently in Foundation custody.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Feeding the cola to lab mice instead of strapping down eight D-Class and forcefeeding them gallons of it before terminating them for no reason? Instant upvote

But srsly I am a sucker for lists of weird things (914, 261, etc)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to believe it's a being of unimaginable power with a mind of a 9 year old being like " oooh this is my new collection" later on "I don't like this, this is my new things I collect" so on and so forth. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't care for the last manifestation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
which I consider ignorant

As a side note, HyaenaBoy's comment was entirely accurate. The author used "race" when they meant "geographic ancestry", and then defended it by linking to a wikipedia article that apparently no one really read, because the right thing to do when you're called out on a technical error is to double down, apparently.

I noticed that as well, but upvoted because I liked it for other reasons and see this kind of sloppy error so often that I barely notice it. Still a valid reason to downvote.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Wandergirl, malicious downvoting is downvoting based on merits other than the content of the piece. Hyaenaboy's comment clearly states the issues he has with the contents of the article, notably tone issues, the DNA mention, and the addendum he didn't like.

Actually, seeing as you've mentioned that your upvote is "countering a downvote", you'd be the one borderline malicious voting, since you're using a basis for your vote that isn't the article's content itself.

Please leave calling out malicious voting to staff. You're free to have your opinion about what you read, but you start to toe the line of rule-breaking when you start attacking others for their opinions.

Edit: Unless I'm missing something, Hyaenaboy novoted on this. You sure you got the right username?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to upvote, if only to counteract HyaenaBoy's downvote, which I consider ignorant and borderline "malicious downvoting". Overall, this thing doesn't rub me the wrong way at any point; if it's turning into a cliché, the most that could do is turn my upvote into a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The crossiants made me happy for some reason :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly for me it was the fact that the researcher writing this report didn't have clearance to fully understand that last manifestation. Wasn't creepy or anything, but it really fits with my head-canon that the only reason things are redacted is because we're all level 2 researchers reading this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the atoms all disappeared like that, they might do something more spectacular than just keel over.

item.title: Agreed
item.content:encoded: He/she redacted the abilities the SCP's did. Kinda made that last bit suck for me. It did not need to be redacted, could the author please tell us what abilities the SCP's had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fourthing.

I was constantly thinking back to math 101 thought exercises where the prof tried to impress on us the true scale of numbers by pointing out how a billion of (random object) would be a pile larger than a football stadium, or Rhode Island, or the Earth (depending on the object).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, this is exactly why I upvoted it. Thank you for putting it into words in a better way than my imagination allowed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FullHazard, you perfectly sum up why I liked this idea. I can certainly understand why not everyone likes it and why people were hoping for a big payoff, and I apologize for disappointing them. But for me the idea of seeing a gigantic pile of billions of croissants and just sitting there going "What the….?" and wondering at the sheer incomprehensibility of it is pretty appealing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got no issues with using human test subjects when it makes sense to do so, but I subscribe to the "Foundation is callous, not evil" territory. Using humans for a potentially-dangerous test in which mice will do just fine is well into evil territory, IMHO.

Edit: And, of course, I mean "in which mice will do just fine" in the narrative sense as well — I've got no issues with using human subjects if it results in more entertaining read, even when there's no practical reason not to use animals. In this case, I personally felt like it would break my suspension of disbelief to use humans on a frivolous test like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't understand this, frankly. I'm not saying that some races are better than others. I'm not saying that your race is a significant factor in your overall abilities, or even that it has any significance at all beyond mere scientific curiosity. What I am saying is that A) races exist, B) races can be determined by examining genes, and C) scientists given a large pile of ears might be curious about how many different populations they represented. (A) and (B) are simple facts (it is worth noting that agreement between self-reported race and DNA determination of your race is higher than agreement between self-reported gender and DNA determination of your gender). (C) seems pretty easy to accept. How are any of these assertions even the tiniest itty-bittiest bit controversial?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't an island that 'makes weird things in weird quantities', it's an island that picks specific traits, and then makes all of the thing thats have those traits.

For example: it didn't make 3-6 billion bibles. It made a copy of every bible ever printed ever. A quick google search gets me two estimates, one at 2.5 billion bibles and one at 7 billion, which is nice and ballparky. Same with human ears: 60 billion is roughly half of the people who have ever lived by some estimates, so it would be easy to say 'this is the left ear of every male human to ever live'.

It brought every Saturn V rocket ever built, and every English Castle. The idea that it's doing something that's entirely reasonable, but at the same time gigantic and ineffable, earns my +1.

(Also it might just be an island that's possessed by a bad science documentary writer. "If you took all the croissants eaten every year, they would make a pile covering an island in the south pacific to a depth of almost sixty meters!")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We have Class Ds to test food on…

Still not bad. Take my Upvote.

item.title: Oh god, someone's console-spamming IRL
item.content:encoded:
Instantly made me think of console-spamming game objects. Upvote!
See?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a bad start… it's interesting, but I don't find myself being grabbed…

I wish I could do a +0.5

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An good start, would appreciate more anomalous objects. The waste place teleporting unused stuff is a good example. No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The objects appear instantaneously and silently, and all objects remaining from the previous manifestation simultaneously vanish.

What about non-manifested objects which were introduced by outsiders? Do they disappear, or are they just buried under the new objects.

For small classes with a few well-known individual instances, the manifested objects have always been observed to include every instance of that object class which is known to have existed.

Copies of the individual instances, or the instances themselves? If not copies, and there's only a few, then it just makes a tiny pile?

Every known British castle was represented, along with five unknown castles which may represent previously-destroyed instances.

"When I started here, all there was was swamp. Other kings said I was daft to build a castle on a swamp, but I built it all the same, just to show 'em. It sank into the swamp. So, I built a second one. That sank into the swamp. So, I built a third one. That burned down, fell over, then sank into the swamp, …"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would personally suggest the line regarding race be scrapped altogether. Also, if anyone's looking for a good reference on the subject, I'd recommend PBS. http://www.pbs.org/race/000_About/002_04-experts-01.htm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it enough to upvote. The idea of an island capable of producing every instance of a class of objects is surreal, which is a good thing to have here. It does, however, lack a substantial WOW moment of realization.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I find this execution very hard to take seriously. The beginning (especially with the image) showed some promise, but I have no investment in just an island that produces absurd numbers of random items every five days regardless of conditions. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since living creatures aren't affected I assumed it was all ears not currently in use. As for Windsor Castle, there was that one time in on spring break…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm okay with this one. It needs some polishing, but I do love it when the image caption makes me want to read further to find out just what's going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, do YOU remember that time that you lost your left ear? Or that other time when Windsor Castle disappeared?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're trying to convince me to vote positively, you've failed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen so many "SCP that causes random shit to appear at fixed intervals" articles at this point that I just automatically groan and downvote when I see a new one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Point of clarification: does island produce the originals, or duplicates? The tagging suggest duplicates, but it's not clearly stated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, was typing in a hurry. Obviously meant descent.

And you are correct, there is no single trait or allele which unambiguously specifies someone's race. There are, however, useful regional averages. Maybe 60% of the people from West Africa have a particular variation of a gene, and only 40% of Eastern Europeans have that allele. That single allele by itself is almost useless — knowing that a person has that variation does not tell you they are from West Africa. But if you look at hundreds of such genes, and the Western African versions of them heavily predominate, you can have very high confidence that you are dealing with a West African individual. I know there's been a trend lately of people claiming "Race isn't genetic!", but that's so misleading that I'd outright call it a lie. We can easily tell someone's race by looking at their DNA. What we can't do is tell their race by looking at individual genes, because there is no allele that all Asians have but no Caucasians have.

The Wikipedia article on Race and Genetics looks to be a decent overview of the subject.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whether "race" is a valid scientific description depends entirely on field. Ethnicity is not the correct term; that's a sociology concept and doesn't apply to ears. The strict biological definition of "race", if I remember right, is strictly in-species (ie, Neanderthals would not apply), but also implies more variation than we tend to see between humans (think dog breeds)- by that definition you're both wrong.

However, consider also http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Race_and_genetics
Only had time to skim it, but what I did read suggests that race may be an appropriate term here, albeit not in the sense that most of us would assume it to mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DNA analysis can certainly distinguish people of, say, African decent from people of Asian decent.

*descent?

I'm a little confused, to be honest. My understanding was that through DNA analysis, you can determine where someone's ancestors came from, but there is no standalone DNA sequence that would determine the race of an individual.

…I've written and rewritten this comment too many times…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of course ethnicity does not equal race — who said it did? Ethnicity is your cultural heritage, race is your genetic heritage. DNA analysis can certainly distinguish people of, say, African decent from people of Asian decent. And Neanderthals are, of course, generally considered to be a different species in the genus Homo, rather than a race of Homo sapiens.

Frankly, I'm not sure exactly what you're complaining about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DNA analysis found normal human DNA representing individuals of a variety of races.

Ethnicity does not equal race. When I saw variety of races, I immediately believe we had found neanderthal ears or something like that. But, that would be an outlier that would exist outside of "normal human DNA".

There are other tone issues that I have trouble believing that scientists would commit, and the last addendum is just completely retarded. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really boring when you consider that it's just a random-item-generator in the form of an island. It's decently written, but no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My reaction is similar to Scantron's: I like it well enough not to downvote, and the writing is fine, but it doesn't really have much of a personality: I don't know why it's producing the things it produces, why it makes so many of them, why the interval between new manifestations is so consistent, how it chooses what to produce next, how and why it exists in the first place, etc.

Not that I'm saying all of those need to be answered or hinted at in the article necessarily; it probably works better if some questions remain open. On the other hand, without any of them, it's just a random thing that makes random stuff for no particular reason. It definitely has potential, but I'm not upvoting yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's just me but I really didn't like that last SCP manifestation, it felt like the standard 'climax-being-last-item-in-list' entry and it didn't really add anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Date: ██/██/████
Objects Observed: Unknown
Notes: The previous manifestation disappeared as expected, but no new manifestation was discovered. It has been suggested that a manifestation may have in fact occurred, but the object or objects were small enough or inconspicuous enough to have escaped notice.

It manifested a colorless gas.. Obviously, guh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, it was plenty clear in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that's exactly what happened — I assumed people would come to that conclusion, but I can make it more obvious if needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like more hint as to what killed them. Did the mice integrate the sugars into their bodies' ATP, only to have the very atoms vanish all at once? That could leave some unique, bizarro effect that an autopsy would reveal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most people don't drink ONLY coke for five days straight (I don't remember if the article mentioned they were also allowed food, if not that's even worse). I mean, if there isn't a problem here and I'm just assuming wrong then ignore this part.

Ultimately I don't hate the writing, but I was waiting your something amazing to come and nothing came. Some of the events seem like they don't add anything in particular to the article, like coissants and bibles.if something doesn't add to the article they weigh it down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After seeing the piles of hate on the other pages in the recently created queue, I'm strangely pleased with a "Well, it doesn't completely suck" :-).

I really wouldn't have thought that five straight days of Coke would have any serious ill effects — I mean, I know humans that more or less do that. As long as their total intake of calories was kept constant, it wouldn't even lead to obesity. I'm not saying it's a great long-term idea, but what ill effects would it have after only a few days?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was mildly interesting to read and well-written, and I read the whole way through without it feeling like slogging at any point, but it doesn't really grab me strongly. The effect isn't ultimately interesting.

As a side note, I would expect mice to see some negative effects from only drinking Coke for five days straight. Maybe change that line to "no unexpected ill effects".
